content:
# Exodus Chapter 32 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~52v–53v (PDF pp. 105–107); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 75–76 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_032_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_032_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_032_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_032_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_032_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE GOLDEN CALF:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 32 across three folio pages in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This is one of the most dramatic and theologically dense chapters in the entire Torah. While Moses lingers on Sinai receiving the tablets written by God's own finger (31:18), the people below shatter the covenant before they have even received it. The chapter moves through five scenes: the people's demand and Aaron's compliance (vv. 1-6), YHWH's wrath and Moses' intercession (vv. 7-14), the descent and shattering of the tablets (vv. 15-20), Aaron's excuse (vv. 21-24), the Levites' slaughter (vv. 25-29), and Moses' second intercession with YHWH's response (vv. 30-35). The verb nikham ("to relent") in v. 14, the act of grinding the calf to powder in v. 20, and Moses' extraordinary offer to be blotted from YHWH's book in v. 32 constitute some of the most theologically weighty moments in the Hebrew Bible. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 75-76) follows the MT closely but contains several notable interpretive shifts, particularly in rendering divine relenting as propitiation.

---

## The People's Demand and the Golden Calf (vv. 1-6)

**1** And the people [va-yar ha-am] saw [ki] that [boshesh] Moses [Mosheh] delayed [la-redet] to come down [min-ha-har] from the mountain; and the people [va-yiqqahel ha-am] gathered themselves [al] against Aaron [Aharon], and they said [va-yomeru] to him [elav]: Arise [qum], make [aseh] for us [lanu] gods [elohim] who [asher] will go [yelkhu] before us [lefaneinu]; for [ki] this [zeh] Moses [Mosheh], the man [ha-ish] who [asher] brought us up [he'elanu] from the land [me-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim] — we do not know [lo yadanu] what [meh] has become [hayah] of him [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּרְא הָעָם כִּי־בֹשֵׁשׁ מֹשֶׁה לָרֶדֶת מִן־הָהָר וַיִּקָּהֵל הָעָם עַל־אַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו קוּם עֲשֵׂה־לָּנוּ אֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר יֵלְכוּ לְפָנֵינוּ כִּי־זֶה מֹשֶׁה הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלָנוּ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לֹא יָדַעְנוּ מֶה־הָיָה לוֹ. **Key terms:** בֹשֵׁשׁ (boshesh) = "delayed, was ashamed to come" — a polel form of בושׁ (bosh, "to be ashamed, to delay"); the word carries overtones of both shame and lateness, as if Moses' absence is itself an embarrassment. וַיִּקָּהֵל (va-yiqqahel) = "and they gathered themselves" — niphal of קהל (qahal, "to assemble"); the people form a qahal, a formal assembly, against Aaron — this is not a mob but an organized demand. אֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר יֵלְכוּ לְפָנֵינוּ (elohim asher yelkhu lefaneinu) = "gods who will go before us" — the plural verb yelkhu with elohim indicates the people are requesting visible deities to replace Moses as mediator; they want a god they can follow with their eyes.

**2** And Aaron [va-yomer Aharon] said [alehem] to them: Break off [parqu] the rings [nizme] of gold [ha-zahav] which [asher] are in the ears [be-oznei] of your wives [nesheikhem], your sons [beneikhem], and your daughters [u-venoteikhem], and bring [ve-havi'u] them to me [elai].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם אַהֲרֹן פָּרְקוּ נִזְמֵי הַזָּהָב אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵי נְשֵׁיכֶם בְּנֵיכֶם וּבְנֹתֵיכֶם וְהָבִיאוּ אֵלָי. **Key terms:** פָּרְקוּ (parqu) = "break off, tear away" — from פרק (paraq, "to tear off, remove by force"); the verb implies violence, a ripping away. נִזְמֵי הַזָּהָב (nizme ha-zahav) = "the rings of gold" — these are the very ornaments plundered from the Egyptians (12:35-36); the gold given by God's providence for the tabernacle (25:3) is now redirected to an idol.

**3** And all the people [va-yitparqu kol-ha-am] broke off [et-nizme] the rings [ha-zahav] of gold [asher] which [be-ozneihem] were in their ears [va-yavi'u], and they brought [el] them to Aaron [Aharon].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּתְפָּרְקוּ כָּל־הָעָם אֶת־נִזְמֵי הַזָּהָב אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶל־אַהֲרֹן. **Key terms:** וַיִּתְפָּרְקוּ (va-yitparqu) = "and they broke off from themselves" — hithpael of פרק, reflexive: they tore the rings from their own ears. The text says בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם (be-ozneihem) = "in their ears" — masculine plural suffix, suggesting the men gave their own earrings rather than collecting from wives and children as Aaron had instructed; the eagerness exceeded the request.

**4** And he took [va-yiqqakh] from their hand [mi-yadam], and he fashioned [va-yatsar] it [oto] with a graving tool [ba-kheret], and he made it [va-ya'asehu] a calf [egel] of cast metal [massekhah]; and they said [va-yomeru]: These [elleh] are your gods [elohekha], O Israel [Yisrael], who [asher] brought you up [he'elukha] from the land [me-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקַּח מִיָּדָם וַיָּצַר אֹתוֹ בַּחֶרֶט וַיַּעֲשֵׂהוּ עֵגֶל מַסֵּכָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלֶּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלוּךָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. **Key terms:** בַּחֶרֶט (ba-kheret) = "with a graving tool, with a stylus" — from חרט (kharat, "to engrave"); the instrument of a craftsman. וַיָּצַר (va-yatsar) = "and he fashioned/formed" — the same verb (יצר, yatsar) used for YHWH forming man from the dust (Gen 2:7); Aaron plays creator in grotesque parody. עֵגֶל מַסֵּכָה (egel massekhah) = "a calf of cast metal" — from נסך (nasakh, "to pour out, cast"); the egel is a young bull, likely evoking the Egyptian Apis bull and the Canaanite imagery of El or Baal as a bull. The LXX reads μόσχον χωνευτόν ("a molten calf"). אֵלֶּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (elleh elohekha) = "these are your gods" — the same words Jeroboam will repeat at Dan and Bethel (1 Kgs 12:28), making this the archetypal act of Israelite apostasy.

**5** And Aaron [va-yar] saw [Aharon], and he built [va-yiven] an altar [mizbeakh] before it [lefanav]; and Aaron [va-yiqra] called out [Aharon] and said [va-yomer]: A feast [khag] to YHWH [la-YHWH] tomorrow [makhar].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּרְא אַהֲרֹן וַיִּבֶן מִזְבֵּחַ לְפָנָיו וַיִּקְרָא אַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר חַג לַיהוָה מָחָר. **Key terms:** חַג לַיהוָה (khag la-YHWH) = "a feast to YHWH" — Aaron attempts to redirect the idolatry by declaring the feast in YHWH's name, as though the calf is merely an image representing YHWH rather than a replacement deity. This syncretism — worshiping YHWH through a forbidden image — is precisely what the second commandment prohibits (20:4-5). The LXX reads ἑορτὴ τοῦ κυρίου αὔριον ("a feast of the Lord tomorrow").

**6** And they rose early [va-yashkimu] on the next day [mi-mokharat], and they offered up [va-ya'alu] burnt offerings [olot], and they brought near [va-yaggishu] peace offerings [shelamim]; and the people [va-yeshev ha-am] sat down [le-ekhol] to eat [ve-shato] and to drink, and they rose up [va-yaqumu] to play [le-tsakheq].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּשְׁכִּימוּ מִמָּחֳרָת וַיַּעֲלוּ עֹלֹת וַיַּגִּשׁוּ שְׁלָמִים וַיֵּשֶׁב הָעָם לֶאֱכֹל וְשָׁתוֹ וַיָּקֻמוּ לְצַחֵק. **Key terms:** לְצַחֵק (le-tsakheq) = "to play" — piel infinitive of צחק (tsakhaq, "to laugh, play"); the word carries overtones of sexual play (cf. Gen 26:8 where Isaac is "playing" with Rebekah; Gen 39:14 where Potiphar's wife accuses Joseph). Paul interprets this as idolatrous revelry (1 Cor 10:7). The sequence — sacrifice, eating, drinking, playing — describes a complete pagan festival, a counter-liturgy to the worship YHWH prescribed in chapters 25-31.

---

## YHWH's Wrath and Moses' Intercession (vv. 7-14)

**7** And YHWH [va-yedabber YHWH] spoke [el-Mosheh] to Moses: Go [lekh], go down [red], for [ki] your people [ammekha] have dealt corruptly [shikhet], whom [asher] you brought up [he'eleita] from the land [me-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֶךְ־רֶד כִּי שִׁחֵת עַמְּךָ אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלֵיתָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. **Key terms:** לֶךְ־רֶד (lekh-red) = "go, go down" — two imperatives in rapid succession conveying urgency. עַמְּךָ (ammekha) = "your people" — YHWH disowns Israel, calling them Moses' people rather than "my people" (contrast 3:7, 3:10); this is an act of rhetorical distancing. שִׁחֵת (shikhet) = "he/they dealt corruptly" — piel of שׁחת (shakhat, "to destroy, corrupt, ruin"); the same root used for the corruption before the flood (Gen 6:11-12).

**8** They have turned aside [saru] quickly [maher] from [min] the way [ha-derekh] which [asher] I commanded them [tsivvitim]; they have made [asu] for themselves [lahem] a calf [egel] of cast metal [massekhah], and they have bowed down [va-yishtakhavu] to it [lo], and they have sacrificed [va-yizbekhu] to it [lo], and they have said [va-yomeru]: These [elleh] are your gods [elohekha], O Israel [Yisrael], who [asher] brought you up [he'elukha] from the land [me-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads סָרוּ מַהֵר מִן־הַדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר צִוִּיתִם עָשׂוּ לָהֶם עֵגֶל מַסֵּכָה וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ־לוֹ וַיִּזְבְּחוּ־לוֹ וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלֶּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלוּךָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. **Key terms:** סָרוּ מַהֵר (saru maher) = "they have turned aside quickly" — from סור (sur, "to turn aside, depart"); the adverb מַהֵר (maher, "quickly") intensifies the betrayal — the covenant was barely ratified before it was broken. YHWH repeats the people's own words (v. 4) back to Moses, quoting their blasphemy.

**9** And YHWH [va-yomer YHWH] said [el-Mosheh] to Moses: I have seen [ra'iti] this people [et-ha-am ha-zeh], and behold [ve-hinneh], it [hu] is a people [am] stiff [qesheh] of neck [oref].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה רָאִיתִי אֶת־הָעָם הַזֶּה וְהִנֵּה עַם־קְשֵׁה־עֹרֶף הוּא. **Key terms:** קְשֵׁה־עֹרֶף (qesheh-oref) = "stiff of neck" — from קשׁה (qashah, "to be hard, difficult") + עֹרֶף (oref, "neck, back of the neck"); the image is of an ox that will not turn its head when the yoke is applied. This epithet becomes a defining characterization of Israel throughout the Hebrew Bible (33:3, 5; 34:9; Deut 9:6, 13; 2 Kgs 17:14). The LXX renders σκληροτράχηλος ("hard-necked"), a direct calque.

**10** And now [ve-attah], let me alone [hannikhah li], and let my anger burn [ve-yikhar appi] against them [bahem], and I will consume them [va-akhallem]; and I will make [ve-e'eseh] you [otkha] into a great [gadol] nation [le-goy].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעַתָּה הַנִּיחָה לִּי וְיִחַר־אַפִּי בָהֶם וַאֲכַלֵּם וְאֶעֱשֶׂה אוֹתְךָ לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל. **Key terms:** הַנִּיחָה לִּי (hannikhah li) = "let me alone, leave me be" — hiphil imperative of נוח (nuakh, "to rest"); paradoxically, YHWH asks Moses for permission to destroy, as though Moses' presence or intercession constitutes a restraint on divine wrath. The rabbis note that YHWH's request implicitly invites Moses to intercede — if destruction were final, why ask to be left alone? וַאֲכַלֵּם (va-akhallem) = "and I will consume them" — piel of כלה (kalah, "to finish, consume, destroy"); total annihilation. וְאֶעֱשֶׂה אוֹתְךָ לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל (ve-e'eseh otkha le-goy gadol) = "and I will make you into a great nation" — YHWH offers to restart the Abrahamic promise through Moses alone, as he once narrowed it through Noah. Moses must choose between personal greatness and solidarity with a sinful people.

**11** And Moses [va-yekhal Mosheh] entreated [et-penei] the face of [YHWH] YHWH [Elohav] his God, and he said [va-yomer]: Why [lamah], O YHWH [YHWH], does your anger burn [yekhereh appekha] against your people [be-ammekha] whom [asher] you brought out [hotseta] from the land [me-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim] with great [be-gadol] power [koakh] and with a strong [u-ve-yad] hand [khazaqah]?

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְחַל מֹשֶׁה אֶת־פְּנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהָיו וַיֹּאמֶר לָמָה יְהוָה יֶחֱרֶה אַפְּךָ בְּעַמֶּךָ אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּכֹחַ גָּדוֹל וּבְיָד חֲזָקָה. **Key terms:** וַיְחַל (va-yekhal) = "and he entreated, and he softened" — piel of חלה (khalah, "to be sick, to make soft, to entreat"); Moses makes YHWH's face "soft," appealing to the divine countenance. בְּעַמֶּךָ (be-ammekha) = "against your people" — Moses immediately reverses YHWH's disowning rhetoric of v. 7 (ammekha, "your people"); he refuses to accept the transfer and insists they are YHWH's people, not his. This is the first of three arguments Moses will advance.

**12** Why [lamah] should Egypt [Mitsrayim] say [yomeru], saying [le'mor]: For evil [be-ra'ah] he brought them out [hotsi'am], to kill them [la-harog otam] in the mountains [be-harim], and to consume them [u-le-khallotam] from upon [me-al] the face [penei] of the ground [ha-adamah]? Turn [shuv] from the burning [me-kharon] of your anger [appekha], and relent [ve-hinnakhem] concerning [al] the evil [ha-ra'ah] against your people [le-ammekha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לָמָּה יֹאמְרוּ מִצְרַיִם לֵאמֹר בְּרָעָה הוֹצִיאָם לַהֲרֹג אֹתָם בֶּהָרִים וּלְכַלֹּתָם מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה שׁוּב מֵחֲרוֹן אַפֶּךָ וְהִנָּחֵם עַל־הָרָעָה לְעַמֶּךָ. **Key terms:** בְּרָעָה הוֹצִיאָם (be-ra'ah hotsi'am) = "for evil he brought them out" — Moses' second argument: YHWH's reputation among the nations. If YHWH destroys Israel, Egypt will interpret the exodus as malicious rather than salvific. וְהִנָּחֵם (ve-hinnakhem) = "and relent, and be grieved" — niphal imperative of נחם (nakham, "to comfort, relent, repent"); Moses boldly commands YHWH to change his mind. The LXX renders ἵλεως γενοῦ ("become gracious/merciful"), avoiding the attribution of a change of mind to God — a significant theological shift from "relent" to "be merciful."

**13** Remember [zekhor] Abraham [le-Avraham], Isaac [le-Yitskhaq], and Israel [u-le-Yisrael], your servants [avadekha], to whom [asher] you swore [nishbata] by yourself [lahem bakha], and you spoke [va-tedabber] to them [alehem]: I will multiply [arbeh] your seed [et-zar'akhem] as the stars [ke-kokhvei] of the heavens [ha-shamayim], and all [ve-khol] this land [ha-arets ha-zot] that [asher] I have spoken of [amarti] I will give [etten] to your seed [le-zar'akhem], and they shall inherit it [ve-nakhalu] forever [le-olam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads זְכֹר לְאַבְרָהָם לְיִצְחָק וּלְיִשְׂרָאֵל עֲבָדֶיךָ אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּעְתָּ לָהֶם בָּךְ וַתְּדַבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם אַרְבֶּה אֶת־זַרְעֲכֶם כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וְכָל־הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת אֲשֶׁר אָמַרְתִּי אֶתֵּן לְזַרְעֲכֶם וְנָחֲלוּ לְעֹלָם. **Key terms:** זְכֹר (zekhor) = "remember" — Moses' third argument: the patriarchal oath. The imperative zekhor calls YHWH back to his own covenant promises to Abraham (Gen 22:16-17), Isaac (Gen 26:3-4), and Jacob/Israel (Gen 35:11-12). נִשְׁבַּעְתָּ לָהֶם בָּךְ (nishbata lahem bakha) = "you swore to them by yourself" — since no one is greater than YHWH, he swore by himself (Gen 22:16; Heb 6:13); breaking this oath would be self-contradiction. Moses' intercession thus rests on three pillars: YHWH's ownership of the people (v. 11), YHWH's reputation among the nations (v. 12), and YHWH's oath to the patriarchs (v. 13).

**14** And YHWH [va-yinnakhem] relented [YHWH] concerning [al] the evil [ha-ra'ah] which [asher] he spoke [dibber] of doing [la'asot] to his people [le-ammo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה עַל־הָרָעָה אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר לַעֲשׂוֹת לְעַמּוֹ. **Key terms:** וַיִּנָּחֶם (va-yinnakhem) = "and he relented" — niphal of נחם (nakham); this is one of the most theologically significant verbs in the Hebrew Bible. YHWH changes his declared intention in response to human intercession. The same verb describes YHWH's grief before the flood (Gen 6:6) and his relenting after Jonah's preaching (Jonah 3:10). לְעַמּוֹ (le-ammo) = "to his people" — the chapter quietly resolves the ownership dispute: Israel is again "his people," not Moses' people. The LXX renders ἱλάσθη κύριος ("the Lord was propitiated"), introducing a sacrificial/atonement concept absent from the Hebrew.

---

## The Descent and the Shattering of the Tablets (vv. 15-20)

**15** And Moses [va-yifen] turned [va-yered] and went down [Mosheh] from [min] the mountain [ha-har], and the two [u-shnei] tablets [lukhot] of the testimony [ha-edut] were in his hand [be-yado] — tablets [lukhot] written [ketuvim] on both [mi-shnei] their sides [evreihem]; from this side [mi-zeh] and from that side [u-mi-zeh] they were written [hem ketuvim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּפֶן וַיֵּרֶד מֹשֶׁה מִן־הָהָר וּשְׁנֵי לֻחֹת הָעֵדֻת בְּיָדוֹ לֻחֹת כְּתֻבִים מִשְּׁנֵי עֶבְרֵיהֶם מִזֶּה וּמִזֶּה הֵם כְּתֻבִים. **Key terms:** מִשְּׁנֵי עֶבְרֵיהֶם (mi-shnei evreihem) = "on both their sides" — the tablets were inscribed on front and back, an unusual feature; ancient Near Eastern documents were typically written on one side only. This detail emphasizes the fullness of the divine communication and the extraordinary craftsmanship of the tablets.

**16** And the tablets [ve-ha-lukhot] — they were the work [ma'aseh] of God [Elohim]; and the writing [ve-ha-mikhtav] — it was the writing [mikhtav] of God [Elohim], engraved [kharut] upon [al] the tablets [ha-lukhot].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהַלֻּחֹת מַעֲשֵׂה אֱלֹהִים הֵמָּה וְהַמִּכְתָּב מִכְתַּב אֱלֹהִים הוּא חָרוּת עַל־הַלֻּחֹת. **Key terms:** מַעֲשֵׂה אֱלֹהִים (ma'aseh Elohim) = "the work of God" — both the tablets and the writing are divine; the stone itself is God's handiwork, not merely the inscription. חָרוּת (kharut) = "engraved" — from חרת (kharat, "to engrave"); the Mishnah (Avot 6:2) famously plays on this word, reading it as חֵרוּת (kherut, "freedom"): "Read not kharut but kherut, for there is no free person except one who occupies himself with Torah."

**17** And Joshua [va-yishma Yehoshua] heard [et-qol] the sound of [ha-am] the people [be-re'oh] as they shouted, and he said [va-yomer] to Moses [el-Mosheh]: A sound [qol] of war [milkhamah] is in the camp [ba-makhaneh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּשְׁמַע יְהוֹשֻׁעַ אֶת־קוֹל הָעָם בְּרֵעֹה וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־מֹשֶׁה קוֹל מִלְחָמָה בַּמַּחֲנֶה. **Key terms:** בְּרֵעֹה (be-re'oh) = "as they shouted, in their shouting" — from רוע (rua', "to shout, raise a cry"); the infinitive construct with בְּ indicates the circumstance of the hearing. Joshua, who had accompanied Moses partway up the mountain (24:13), has been waiting and now hears the distant commotion. בַּמַּחֲנֶה (ba-makhaneh) = "in the camp" — Joshua misidentifies the sound as battle; Moses will correct him.

**18** And he said [va-yomer]: It is not [ein] a sound [qol] of the cry [anot] of might [gevurah], and it is not [ve-ein] a sound [qol] of the cry [anot] of weakness [khalushah]; a sound [qol] of singing [annot] I [anokhi] hear [shome'a].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֵין קוֹל עֲנוֹת גְּבוּרָה וְאֵין קוֹל עֲנוֹת חֲלוּשָׁה קוֹל עַנּוֹת אָנֹכִי שֹׁמֵעַ. **Key terms:** עֲנוֹת...עֲנוֹת...עַנּוֹת (anot...anot...annot) = a wordplay on three forms of ענה (anah): the first two are infinitives meaning "to cry out" (in victory and defeat), while the third may derive from a different root meaning "to sing, to respond antiphonally." Moses' ear distinguishes what Joshua's cannot — this is not war but revelry. The threefold repetition of qol ("sound") creates a poetic tricolon unique in biblical prose.

**19** And it was [va-yehi], as he drew near [ka-asher qarav] to [el] the camp [ha-makhaneh], that he saw [va-yar] the calf [et-ha-egel] and the dancing [u-mekholot]; and the anger [va-yikhar af] of Moses [Mosheh] burned, and he cast [va-yashlekh] from his hands [mi-yadav] the tablets [et-ha-lukhot], and he shattered them [va-yeshabber otam] beneath [takhat] the mountain [ha-har].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר קָרַב אֶל־הַמַּחֲנֶה וַיַּרְא אֶת־הָעֵגֶל וּמְחֹלֹת וַיִּחַר־אַף מֹשֶׁה וַיַּשְׁלֵךְ מִיָּדָיו אֶת־הַלֻּחֹת וַיְשַׁבֵּר אֹתָם תַּחַת הָהָר. **Key terms:** וּמְחֹלֹת (u-mekholot) = "and the dancing" — from מחול (makhol, "dance"); the sight of the calf alone might have been endured, but the dancing — evidence of wholehearted cultic celebration — ignites Moses' fury. וַיְשַׁבֵּר (va-yeshabber) = "and he shattered" — piel of שׁבר (shavar, "to break"); the intensive form indicates deliberate, forceful destruction, not accidental dropping. Moses shatters the covenant document as a legal act: a broken treaty tablet signifies a broken covenant. The tablets written by God's finger (31:18) are destroyed before Israel ever reads them.

**20** And he took [va-yiqqakh] the calf [et-ha-egel] which [asher] they had made [asu], and he burned [va-yisrof] it in the fire [ba-esh], and he ground [va-yit'an] it until [ad asher] it was fine [daq], and he scattered [va-yizer] it upon [al-penei] the face of the waters [ha-mayim], and he made the sons of Israel [va-yashq et-benei Yisrael] drink it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקַּח אֶת־הָעֵגֶל אֲשֶׁר עָשׂוּ וַיִּשְׂרֹף בָּאֵשׁ וַיִּטְחַן עַד אֲשֶׁר־דָּק וַיִּזֶר עַל־פְּנֵי הַמַּיִם וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל. **Key terms:** וַיִּטְחַן (va-yit'an) = "and he ground" — from טחן (takhan, "to grind"); the calf is reduced to powder, utterly annihilated. The process — burn, grind, scatter on water, force to drink — is methodical destruction that may echo the ordeal of the suspected adulteress (Num 5:24), where the woman drinks water containing dissolved curse-ink. Israel has committed spiritual adultery, and Moses administers the bitter water. וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (va-yashq et-benei Yisrael) = "and he made the sons of Israel drink" — hiphil of שׁקה (shaqah, "to give drink"); they must internalize the evidence of their sin.

## Aaron's Excuse (vv. 21-24)

**21** And Moses [va-yomer Mosheh] said [el-Aharon] to Aaron: What [meh] did this people [ha-am ha-zeh] do [asah] to you [lekha] that [ki] you brought [heveta] upon them [alav] a great [gedolah] sin [khata'ah]?

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן מֶה־עָשָׂה לְךָ הָעָם הַזֶּה כִּי־הֵבֵאתָ עָלָיו חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה. **Key terms:** חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה (khata'ah gedolah) = "a great sin" — the same phrase used for adultery in Gen 20:9 and sexual sin in Gen 39:9; the golden calf is covenant adultery. Moses' question implies that only extreme duress could explain Aaron's compliance.

**22** And Aaron [va-yomer Aharon] said: Let not [al] the anger [af] of my lord [adoni] burn [yikhar]; you [attah] know [yadata] the people [et-ha-am], that [ki] they are [hu] in evil [be-ra].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אַהֲרֹן אַל־יִחַר אַף אֲדֹנִי אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ אֶת־הָעָם כִּי בְרָע הוּא. **Key terms:** כִּי בְרָע הוּא (ki ve-ra hu) = "that they are in evil" or "that it is set on evil" — Aaron deflects blame to the people's character, a strategy of excuse-making that contrasts sharply with Moses' willingness to bear responsibility (v. 32).

**23** And they said [va-yomeru] to me [li]: Make [aseh] for us [lanu] gods [elohim] who [asher] will go [yelkhu] before us [lefaneinu]; for [ki] this [zeh] Moses [Mosheh], the man [ha-ish] who [asher] brought us up [he'elanu] from the land [me-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim] — we do not know [lo yadanu] what [meh] has become [hayah] of him [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמְרוּ לִי עֲשֵׂה־לָנוּ אֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר יֵלְכוּ לְפָנֵינוּ כִּי־זֶה מֹשֶׁה הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלָנוּ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לֹא יָדַעְנוּ מֶה־הָיָה לוֹ. **Key terms:** Aaron accurately quotes the people's demand from v. 1, demonstrating that he remembers the words perfectly even as he minimizes his own agency in what followed.

**24** And I said [va-omar] to them [lahem]: Who [mi] has gold [zahav]? Break it off [hitparqu]; and they gave [va-yittenu] it to me [li], and I cast it [va-ashlikhehu] into the fire [ba-esh], and this calf [va-yetse ha-egel ha-zeh] came out.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וָאֹמַר לָהֶם לְמִי זָהָב הִתְפָּרָקוּ וַיִּתְּנוּ־לִי וָאַשְׁלִכֵהוּ בָאֵשׁ וַיֵּצֵא הָעֵגֶל הַזֶּה. **Key terms:** וַיֵּצֵא הָעֵגֶל הַזֶּה (va-yetse ha-egel ha-zeh) = "and this calf came out" — the most transparent lie in the Torah. Aaron claims the calf spontaneously emerged from the fire, omitting entirely his role in fashioning it with a graving tool (v. 4). The verb יצא (yatsa, "to come out, go out") portrays the calf as self-generating, as if the gold animated itself. The narrative's deadpan recording of this excuse, without comment, is devastating irony.

---

## The Levites' Sword (vv. 25-29)

**25** And Moses [va-yar Mosheh] saw [et-ha-am] the people, that [ki] it was let loose [farua hu] — for [ki] Aaron [pe-ra'oh] had let them loose [Aharon] as a whispered shame [le-shimtsah] among those who rose against them [be-qamehem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּרְא מֹשֶׁה אֶת־הָעָם כִּי פָרֻעַ הוּא כִּי־פְרָעֹה אַהֲרֹן לְשִׁמְצָה בְּקָמֵיהֶם. **Key terms:** פָרֻעַ (farua) = "let loose, unbridled, unrestrained" — from פרע (para', "to let loose, let go, uncover"); the same root used for unbound hair (Lev 13:45; Num 5:18) and for lack of prophetic vision (Prov 29:18). The people are out of control — morally and physically undone. לְשִׁמְצָה (le-shimtsah) = "as a whispered shame, as an object of derision" — a rare word appearing only here and in 2 Chr 29:8; Israel has become a scandal among watching enemies. בְּקָמֵיהֶם (be-qamehem) = "among those who rose against them" — their adversaries now have cause to mock. The LXX renders διεσκέδασται ("scattered/dispersed"), interpreting the looseness as disorder.

**26** And Moses [va-ya'amod Mosheh] stood [be-sha'ar] in the gate [ha-makhaneh] of the camp, and he said [va-yomer]: Who [mi] is for YHWH [la-YHWH]? — to me [elai]! And all [va-ye'asfu elav kol] the sons [benei] of Levi [Levi] gathered to him.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעֲמֹד מֹשֶׁה בְּשַׁעַר הַמַּחֲנֶה וַיֹּאמֶר מִי לַיהוָה אֵלָי וַיֵּאָסְפוּ אֵלָיו כָּל־בְּנֵי לֵוִי. **Key terms:** מִי לַיהוָה אֵלָי (mi la-YHWH elai) = "Who is for YHWH? — to me!" — one of the most dramatic calls to allegiance in the Hebrew Bible; Moses forces an immediate, public choice. כָּל־בְּנֵי לֵוִי (kol-benei Levi) = "all the sons of Levi" — the entire tribe responds, foreshadowing their later consecration as the priestly tribe. Their loyalty here earns what follows in v. 29.

**27** And he said [va-yomer] to them [lahem]: Thus [koh] says [amar] YHWH [YHWH], the God [Elohei] of Israel [Yisrael]: Put [simu], each man [ish], his sword [kharbo] upon [al] his thigh [yerekho]; pass [ivru] and return [va-shuvu] from gate [mi-sha'ar] to gate [le-sha'ar] in the camp [ba-makhaneh], and kill [ve-hirgu], each man [ish], his brother [et-akhiv], and each man [ve-ish] his companion [et-re'ehu], and each man [ve-ish] his neighbor [et-qerovo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם כֹּה־אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל שִׂימוּ אִישׁ־חַרְבּוֹ עַל־יְרֵכוֹ עִבְרוּ וָשׁוּבוּ מִשַּׁעַר לָשַׁעַר בַּמַּחֲנֶה וְהִרְגוּ אִישׁ־אֶת־אָחִיו וְאִישׁ אֶת־רֵעֵהוּ וְאִישׁ אֶת־קְרֹבוֹ. **Key terms:** אִישׁ־אֶת־אָחִיו...אֶת־רֵעֵהוּ...אֶת־קְרֹבוֹ (ish et-akhiv...et-re'ehu...et-qerovo) = "each man his brother...his companion...his neighbor" — the three terms cover every category of social relationship; the judgment does not spare kinship, friendship, or proximity. עִבְרוּ וָשׁוּבוּ (ivru va-shuvu) = "pass and return" — a systematic sweep through the camp, not random violence.

**28** And the sons [va-ya'asu] of Levi [benei-Levi] did [ki-dvar] according to the word [Mosheh] of Moses; and there fell [va-yippol] from the people [min-ha-am] on that day [ba-yom ha-hu] about three [ki-shloshet] thousand [alfe] men [ish].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בְנֵי־לֵוִי כִּדְבַר מֹשֶׁה וַיִּפֹּל מִן־הָעָם בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא כִּשְׁלֹשֶׁת אַלְפֵי אִישׁ. **Key terms:** כִּשְׁלֹשֶׁת אַלְפֵי אִישׁ (ki-shloshet alfe ish) = "about three thousand men" — the כ (ki, "about") indicates an approximation. The LXX reads εἰς τρισχιλίους ἄνδρας ("about three thousand men"), matching the MT.

**29** And Moses [va-yomer Mosheh] said: Fill [mill'u] your hand [yedkhem] today [ha-yom] for YHWH [la-YHWH] — for [ki] each man [ish] against his son [bi-vno] and against his brother [u-ve-akhiv] — and to give [ve-latet] upon you [aleikhem] today [ha-yom] a blessing [berakhah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה מִלְאוּ יֶדְכֶם הַיּוֹם לַיהוָה כִּי אִישׁ בִּבְנוֹ וּבְאָחִיו וְלָתֵת עֲלֵיכֶם הַיּוֹם בְּרָכָה. **Key terms:** מִלְאוּ יֶדְכֶם (mill'u yedkhem) = "fill your hand" — the idiom for consecration to priestly service (literally "fill the hand," as in ordination, cf. 28:41; 29:9); by executing judgment, the Levites are ordained. Their willingness to place YHWH above family loyalty consecrates them for service. בְּרָכָה (berakhah) = "a blessing" — the grim act of judgment paradoxically yields blessing; covenant fidelity, even at terrible cost, is the path to divine favor. The LXX renders ἐπληρώσατε τὰς χεῖρας ὑμῶν ("fill your hands"), preserving the literal idiom.

## Moses' Second Intercession and the Divine Response (vv. 30-35)

**30** And it was [va-yehi] on the next day [mi-mokharat], and Moses [va-yomer Mosheh] said [el-ha-am] to the people: You [attem] have sinned [khatat'em] a great [gedolah] sin [khata'ah]; and now [ve-attah] I will go up [e'eleh] to [el] YHWH [YHWH] — perhaps [ulai] I can make atonement [akhapperah] for [be'ad] your sin [khattat'khem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי מִמָּחֳרָת וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם אַתֶּם חֲטָאתֶם חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה וְעַתָּה אֶעֱלֶה אֶל־יְהוָה אוּלַי אֲכַפְּרָה בְּעַד חַטַּאתְכֶם. **Key terms:** אוּלַי (ulai) = "perhaps" — Moses offers no guarantee of success; the word ulai expresses genuine uncertainty about whether atonement is possible. אֲכַפְּרָה (akhapperah) = "I can make atonement, I can cover" — piel of כפר (kaphar, "to cover, atone, ransom"); the same root as kapporet ("mercy seat," 25:17). Moses will attempt to do in intercession what the mercy seat does in ritual: cover sin before the divine presence.

**31** And Moses [va-yashov Mosheh] returned [el-YHWH] to YHWH, and he said [va-yomer]: Alas [anna], this people [ha-am ha-zeh] has sinned [khata] a great [gedolah] sin [khata'ah], and they have made [va-ya'asu] for themselves [lahem] gods [elohei] of gold [zahav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּשָׁב מֹשֶׁה אֶל־יְהוָה וַיֹּאמַר אָנָּא חָטָא הָעָם הַזֶּה חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה וַיַּעֲשׂוּ לָהֶם אֱלֹהֵי זָהָב. **Key terms:** אָנָּא (anna) = "alas, please, I beseech" — an interjection of urgent pleading, used in the most desperate prayers in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ps 116:4; Jonah 1:14; 4:2). Moses does not minimize the sin but confesses it fully before pleading for mercy.

**32** And now [ve-attah], if [im] you will forgive [tissa] their sin [khattatam] — and if [ve-im] not [ayin], blot me out [mekheni na] from your book [mi-sifrekha] which [asher] you have written [katavta].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעַתָּה אִם־תִּשָּׂא חַטָּאתָם וְאִם־אַיִן מְחֵנִי נָא מִסִּפְרְךָ אֲשֶׁר כָּתָבְתָּ. **Key terms:** אִם־תִּשָּׂא (im-tissa) = "if you will forgive" — literally "if you will carry/lift" their sin; the verb נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, carry, bear") when used with sin means to bear it away, to forgive. The sentence breaks off after tissa — the apodosis is missing, creating a deliberate aposiopesis: "If you will forgive their sin — [well and good]; and if not..." Moses cannot bring himself to complete the first clause; the alternative overwhelms him. מְחֵנִי נָא מִסִּפְרְךָ (mekheni na mi-sifrekha) = "blot me out, please, from your book" — Moses offers his own life and eternal destiny as substitute for Israel. This is the most radical intercessory act in the Hebrew Bible. סִפְרְךָ אֲשֶׁר כָּתָבְתָּ (sifrekha asher katavta) = "your book which you have written" — the book of life, a divine register of the living (cf. Ps 69:29; Dan 12:1; Mal 3:16); to be blotted out is to cease to exist before God. This is the first mention of a divine "book" (sefer) of life in the Torah.

**33** And YHWH [va-yomer YHWH] said [el-Mosheh] to Moses: Whoever [mi asher] has sinned [khata] against me [li], I will blot him out [emkhennu] from my book [mi-sifri].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה מִי אֲשֶׁר חָטָא־לִי אֶמְחֶנּוּ מִסִּפְרִי. **Key terms:** YHWH refuses Moses' substitutionary offer. Divine justice is individual: each person bears their own sin. Moses cannot die for Israel's transgression. The verb אֶמְחֶנּוּ (emkhennu) = "I will blot him out" — the same root מחה (makhah) Moses used in v. 32, now turned back on the guilty individuals rather than accepted as collective substitution.

**34** And now [ve-attah], go [lekh], lead [nekheh] the people [et-ha-am] to [el] the place [asher] of which [dibbarti] I spoke [lakh] to you. Behold [hinneh], my angel [malakhi] will go [yelekh] before you [lefanekha]; and on the day [u-ve-yom] of my visiting [poqdi], I will visit [u-faqadti] upon them [alehem] their sin [khattatam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעַתָּה לֵךְ נְחֵה אֶת־הָעָם אֶל אֲשֶׁר־דִּבַּרְתִּי לָךְ הִנֵּה מַלְאָכִי יֵלֵךְ לְפָנֶיךָ וּבְיוֹם פָּקְדִי וּפָקַדְתִּי עֲלֵהֶם חַטָּאתָם. **Key terms:** מַלְאָכִי יֵלֵךְ לְפָנֶיךָ (malakhi yelekh lefanekha) = "my angel will go before you" — a significant diminishment: YHWH will send an angel rather than going himself (cf. 33:2-3), a consequence of the sin that will not be fully resolved until 34:9. וּבְיוֹם פָּקְדִי וּפָקַדְתִּי (u-ve-yom poqdi u-faqadti) = "and on the day of my visiting, I will visit" — the infinitive construct with cognate finite verb emphasizes certainty; divine punishment is deferred but not canceled. The verb פקד (paqad, "to visit, attend to, reckon") carries both positive and negative senses; here it is punitive. Forgiveness has come, but consequences remain. The LXX renders ἐν ᾗ ἡμέρᾳ ἐπισκέπτωμαι ("on whatever day I visit"), preserving the open-ended threat.

**35** And YHWH [va-yiggof YHWH] struck [et-ha-am] the people because [al asher] they made [asu] the calf [et-ha-egel] which [asher] Aaron [asah] made [Aharon].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּגֹּף יְהוָה אֶת־הָעָם עַל אֲשֶׁר עָשׂוּ אֶת־הָעֵגֶל אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה אַהֲרֹן. **Key terms:** וַיִּגֹּף (va-yiggof) = "and he struck" — from נגף (nagaf, "to strike, plague"); the same verb used for the striking of the Egyptians (12:23, 27). The plague that once fell on Israel's oppressors now falls on Israel itself. עַל אֲשֶׁר עָשׂוּ אֶת־הָעֵגֶל אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה אַהֲרֹן (al asher asu et-ha-egel asher asah Aharon) = "because they made the calf which Aaron made" — the final verse distributes guilt to both the people and Aaron; the double use of עשׂה (asah, "to make") assigns shared responsibility. Aaron's excuse of v. 24 — "and this calf came out" — is silently refuted by the narrator's closing statement: Aaron made it.

---

## Translator's Afterword

Exodus 32 is the catastrophe at the center of the Torah — the shattering of the covenant at the very moment of its inscription. The tablets written by God's finger are broken before Israel reads a word. The chapter stands as the great theological counterweight to Sinai: if chapters 19-24 establish the covenant, chapter 32 tests whether it can survive betrayal.

The opening verb boshesh ("delayed") sets the entire catastrophe in motion. Moses' absence — a mere forty days on the mountain (24:18) — becomes unbearable to a people who cannot conceive of a God they cannot see. Their demand for elohim asher yelkhu lefaneinu ("gods who will go before us") is not atheism but visible religion: they want a god they can follow with their eyes. Aaron's compliance produces an egel massekhah ("calf of cast metal"), a young bull image that evokes the iconography of Egypt (the Apis bull) and Canaan (El and Baal as bull deities). The phrase elleh elohekha Yisrael ("these are your gods, O Israel") will echo across centuries; Jeroboam will repeat it verbatim at Dan and Bethel (1 Kgs 12:28), making the golden calf the template for all subsequent Israelite idolatry.

YHWH's response (vv. 7-10) introduces the stunning rhetorical move of disowning Israel — ammekha ("your people"), he says to Moses, not ammi ("my people"). The offer to destroy Israel and restart the covenant through Moses alone reprises the flood pattern: divine grief, the offer to save one righteous man, the potential to begin again. Moses' intercession (vv. 11-13) rests on three arguments: YHWH's investment in the exodus (v. 11), YHWH's reputation among the nations (v. 12), and YHWH's oath to the patriarchs (v. 13). The climactic result — va-yinnakhem YHWH ("and YHWH relented," v. 14) — is one of the most theologically charged statements in the Hebrew Bible. The niphal of nakham attributes to God a genuine change of stated intention in response to human prayer, a datum that resists every attempt at systematic domestication.

The descent (vv. 15-20) is staged with cinematic precision: the sound misidentified (vv. 17-18), the sight revealed (v. 19), the tablets hurled down (v. 19), the calf destroyed by burning, grinding, dissolving, and forced drinking (v. 20) — a sequence that may deliberately evoke the bitter water ordeal for a wife suspected of adultery (Num 5:11-31). Aaron's excuse (vv. 21-24) is a masterpiece of narrative irony; his claim that the calf "came out" of the fire by itself (va-yetse ha-egel ha-zeh) stands in stark contrast to the narrator's report that Aaron fashioned it with a graving tool (va-yatsar oto ba-kheret, v. 4).

The Levites' sword (vv. 25-29) introduces the grim cost of covenant loyalty. Moses' cry mi la-YHWH elai ("Who is for YHWH? — to me!") forces an immediate choice, and the sons of Levi respond. Their willingness to execute judgment against brother, companion, and neighbor — the three categories of social bond — earns them consecration: mill'u yedkhem ha-yom la-YHWH ("fill your hand today for YHWH"), the ordination formula. The roughly three thousand who fall mark the immediate human cost of the apostasy.

Moses' second intercession (vv. 30-32) reaches the most extreme offer in all of Scripture: mekheni na mi-sifrekha — "blot me out from your book." Moses offers his own existence before God as the price for Israel's forgiveness. The broken conditional of v. 32 — im tissa khattatam, ve-im ayin — leaves the first clause unfinished, one of the very few aposiopeses in the Hebrew Bible; the silence speaks louder than any words Moses could have uttered. YHWH refuses the substitution (v. 33), insisting on individual accountability, but does not abandon the people. The chapter ends with three forms of judgment: the Levites' sword (about three thousand dead, v. 28), the deferred day of visitation (v. 34), and a plague (v. 35). Yet the covenant continues — battered, fractured, but not annulled.

Key MT-LXX divergences: (1) The Vaticanus text follows the MT closely throughout Exodus 32, with no major structural differences. (2) The LXX renders egel massekhah (v. 4) as μόσχον χωνευτόν ("a molten calf"), accurately conveying the cast-metal process. (3) In v. 12, the LXX renders ve-hinnakhem as ἵλεως γενοῦ ("become gracious/merciful"), avoiding the attribution of a change of mind to God — a theologically motivated shift from "relent" to "be merciful." (4) In v. 14, the LXX renders va-yinnakhem as ἱλάσθη κύριος ("the Lord was propitiated"), consistently reinterpreting divine nakham as propitiation rather than a change of purpose. This is the most significant MT-LXX divergence in the chapter: the MT portrays YHWH changing his stated intention, while the LXX portrays YHWH being appeased. (5) In v. 18, the Hebrew's triple wordplay on anot/annot is notoriously difficult; some LXX witnesses read φωνὴ ἐξαρχόντων οἴνου ("a sound of those leading in wine"), a significantly different interpretation. (6) In v. 25, the LXX renders farua as διεσκέδασται ("scattered/dispersed"), interpreting the looseness as physical disorder rather than moral abandon. (7) In v. 29, the LXX renders mill'u yedkhem as ἐπληρώσατε τὰς χεῖρας ὑμῶν ("fill your hands"), preserving the literal ordination idiom. (8) Throughout, YHWH is rendered κύριος in the LXX. (9) The Vaticanus manuscript shows the text in continuous uncial script across three columns on pages 75-76, with paragraph markers (ekthesis) at major section breaks corresponding to the MT's setumah and petukha divisions.
